name: Palo Mayombe
description: Palo Mayombe, a Kongo-derived religion and magic system, is deeply rooted in the beliefs and practices of Central African slaves brought to Cuba. It emphasizes direct engagement with the spirits of the dead (nfumbe) and the forces of nature (nkisi). Unlike its more syncretic cousin Santería, Palo Mayombe retains a stronger connection to its Bantu origins, focusing on the power of the natural world and ancestral spirits. The central ritual object is the nganga or prenda, a consecrated iron pot containing earth, sticks, bones, and other sacred objects, housing a powerful spirit. Paleros, initiates of Palo Mayombe, work with these spirits to bring about change, healing, protection, or even harm. Ethical considerations are paramount, as actions have consequences, and the palero must maintain a balanced relationship with the spirit world. Divination plays a crucial role in understanding the needs and desires of the spirits and guiding ritual actions. The religion is characterized by secrecy, initiation rituals, and a hierarchical structure of knowledge and power. Palo Mayombe is a vibrant and evolving tradition that continues to be practiced in Cuba and throughout the diaspora, offering its followers a path to spiritual empowerment and connection with their ancestors.
subtitle: Power of the Dead and Nature

keyMyths.title: The Creation of the Nganga
keyMyths.description: The Nganga is created through a complex ritual involving the invocation of spirits and the placement of sacred objects within the pot. It represents a microcosm of the universe and a powerful conduit for spiritual energy.

sources.author: David Brown
sources.work: The Light Inside: Abakuá Society Arts and Cuban Cultural History
sources.description: Explores the cultural context and artistic expressions related to Afro-Cuban religions.
